text: cup of coffee and ran to the hall. “Rebecca was looking for you. Hamilton didn’t show, and Weston’s delivering already. You mind taking over a delivery?” “Right now?” “Yeah. They’ve induced, and she’s at seven centimeters.” Stan shook his head at the irony of bringing in life while on the verge of ending his own. “You know I haven’t delivered in years, right?” “It’s like riding a bike.” Greg joked. Stan hesitated, then thought of the patient in room 201 who had yet to wake up and realize he’d lost a leg. He didn’t want to be there when it happened. “Yeah, okay. But you’ve gotta take my rounds.” “Done.” Stan washed, suited up, and had a short discussion with himself in the mirror about keeping his shit together, going through the steps of childbirth. He’d spent so many days among the dead and dying in the past several years that delivering a newborn felt as foreign as taking a boat down the Amazon with local natives. Finish the shift. Then you can go home and do it. Go out knowing you brought something in . He snapped his gloves and pushed elbows through the washroom doors. The nurses stood waiting. There was no father present. No partner at all to help the pregnant woman in her time of need, only the beeping of machines and the probing of stranger’s fingers. One nurse was doing her best to console the woman, who was clearly in pain, while another nurse filled the doctor in with a very unexpected turn of events. “The baby is breech,” she said. “Breech? Nobody told me that. And there’s no mention of it on her—” “It just became breech,” the nurse kept her voice at a whisper, with her back to the woman in labor. “What do you mean, it just became breech?” “I mean, I felt the baby’s head. The mother was dilated up to 9, and when I checked again, there’s his feet.” “So he’s in there doing somersaults?” The nurse shrugged, and Stan made his way to his patient. “Hello, Ms. Salyer. I’m Dr. Lipton. It looks like today’s going to be a very special day for you.” The woman, shiny with sweat, looked at each face in the room, reading them. If this were poker, they’d all lose. “What’s wrong? Something’s wrong, isn’t it?” Stan placed a hand on the woman’s arm. “Nothing we’re not prepared to handle. That’s the good news. The more interesting news is that your little guy has decided to change positions at the last minute.” “Change positions?” she said through ghost-white lips. “Instead of the baby’s head greeting us first, he wants to hit the ground running...feet first.” “He’s breech?” A pained grimace stretched her face as she held her bulging abdomen. “Why didn’t my doctor tell me? That’s not what the ultrasound showed.” “That’s because your baby wasn’t breech then. Somehow he managed to do this in the past hour. Now, do you feel any discomfort outside of the norm? Particularly in the last half hour?” “I feel a whole hell of a lot of discomfort.” The woman winced, squeezing her abdomen. “Okay. How do you feel about a Cesarean birth? There’s a chance we may have to do it anyway, especially since you’ve already been induced, but I think it’s a good idea to start heading in that direction.” Through gritted teeth, the woman said, “That’s fine...whatever it takes.” “Okay, we’re going to get an anesthesiologist in here for you. In the meantime, the nurse is going to prep you, and we’ll insert a catheter just to keep your bladder empty. If you have any questions, Angela here can answer them for you. “ Stan left and went straight for the restroom, where he studied himself in the mirror again as the faucet ran cold water. He held his hand out flat, checked the steadiness, anticipating a tremble, but it was still and level. He looked back to the mirror. “That baby wants nothing to do with this world.” His voice was a stone sailing down an empty well. He zoned out and let a montage of bad memories play on the wall before him—the divorce and all its extra-marital activity, the hundred times he’s delivered bad news to waiting families, the smoking habit he knows damn well he should kick. Several minutes went by before the opening of doors brought him back to reality. “We’re ready, Dr. Lipton,” a nurse said. He washed his hands again, then pushed through the doors, ready to force a human into this shithole. # “I don’t suppose the baby changed his mind,” Dr. Lipton said to the nurse. “Sir?” “Did he flip back around?” “No, Doctor. I don’t think he could have—” “Well, he couldn’t have done what he already did, could he?” The nurse kept silent and seemed to cower. While the mother was given oxygen, Stan made a horizontal incision above the pubic bone. A team of hands kept the area free of blood, stretching the muscle once he’d reached it. Through uneducated eyes, the procedure was brutal, even careless, as the many fingers tugged and stretched the bright red flesh. The opening was kept wide with retractors and fingers, like so many tentacles, and Stan punctured the amniotic sac, destroying the last bit of border between the baby and the world. He pushed his hand through the thick wall of flesh, then gently, but firmly, grabbed both of the baby’s feet. Gloved fingers tugged and widened the opening, making room for the tiny body, while Dr. Lipton led the baby out of its mother. Then stopped. The ease of progress through the slick path came to a halt, as though the baby was no longer cooperating. The doctor pulled again. “Give me some light...right down in there.” One quick moment later, a bright light shined inside the woman. With a determined grip, the baby was holding strong to the far end of its umbilical cord
